title: `podman rm --force` eventually doesn't clean up fully
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

If you have a running container `c` that is exposed on some port, say 8080, and you remove it using `podman rm --force c` and then start it again over and over, eventually `rm --force` will not kill `rootlessport` and port 8080 will remain in use.

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `podman run -d -p 8080:80 --name c docker.io/nginx`
2. `podman rm --force c`
3. `ps -ax`
4. Repeat until process list includes `rootlessport` and some `nginx` stuff

**Describe the results you received:**

A process called `rootlessport` and a bunch of processes related to nginx that are all leftovers from a destroyed container 

**Describe the results you expected:**

No leftovers after removing a container

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

All this was done in a rootless environment with no prior `docker` installation on that machine
Before opening a new issue I left a comment on #6762. It has some additional info as to what originally happened
Using `podman stop c && podman rm c` intead of `podman rm --force c` seems to not be causing any leftovers

**Output of `podman version`:**

```

```

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out, @selamba.

I am unable to reproduce the issue.  How often do you need to repeat the reproducer for the processes to show up?

@vrothberg I suspect it's random. It would probably be more apparent if you tried to create the same container with the same ports exposed without removing the previous one. Originally that's almost what I did - I had a compose file that exposed a port and added the container to a network along with a database container. To restart the container I didn't stop and remove it, I just ran the `podman-compose` command again. After a bunch of such failed `podman-compose`s, I `rm --force` the container. That's when the cleanup failure originally occurred.
i ran this 1200 times in a tight loop and was not able to replicate this.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since we have not heard anything further on this and have not been able to replicate, I am closing.
This is happening to me too while using Ansible [containers.podman.podman_containers](https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/collections/containers/podman/podman_container_module.html)
